item.title: Switch to Claude without starting over | Claude
item.description: Transfer your preferences, projects, and context from other AI providers into Claude. Switch without losing what makes your AI useful.

item.title: Our agreement with the Department of War
item.description: Details on OpenAI’s contract with the Department of War, outlining safety red lines, legal protections, and how AI systems will be deployed in classified environments.

item.title: microgpt
item.description: Musings of a Computer Scientist.

item.title: “How old are you?” Asked the OS
item.description: California is mandating that all operating systems ask for the user's age during account creation. There are so many flaws with this premise. We lose control.

item.title: On How Mailchimp Suspended Our Account · @radekmie’s take on IT and stuff
item.description: Here’s a short horror story of us losing tens of thousands of emails. Hopefully it won’t happen to you, but if you do, here’s a few tips.

item.title: Feature Flags: Power, Risk and Responsibility
item.description: It’s Friday afternoon.You’ve just deployed a new feature.Tests are green. The code review passed. Everything looked fine in staging.Ten minutes later, the fi...

item.title: I built an RGB controller with Arduino – Miloš Švaňa
item.description: Before Christmas, I bought a new computer case. It came with three built-in RGB fans. Unfortunately, the fans use a 3-pin aRGB connection, while my now quite old motherboard has a 4-pin RGB header. I am sure that I could find an adapter that would solve this issue right away. But there is no fun in that. Having experimented with Arduino for some time, now was the time to use my skills to build something useful.

item.title: WebTiles: It's fine to accept user-supplied code, actually
item.description: My website and blog.

item.title: It's incredibly easy to game Twitter's trending news algorithm
item.description: Twitter’s “Today’s News” section is a mix of real news, very minor stories (usually discussion of a random AI-related post), nonsense trends, and barely disguised marketing. The algorithm behind it seems pretty easy to manipulate. This trending topic revolves around an explosive DM warning of imminent 25% layoffs at a FAANG company: Here is the original post, which comes from an account called Tech Layoff Tracker (@TechLayoffLover): There is no reason to believe this post is real. The account, created this month (February 2026), made its first post 7 hours ago. The post in question was made 5 hours ago, or 2 hours after the account’s very first post. Of course, the account carries an utterly meaningless blue “verified” checkmark.

item.title: “They’ll be bored of the slop soon enough” – Writing for Design Jury President Holly Kielty
item.description: Published

item.title: On Beads, Bloat, and Breaking Points
item.description: Beads changed how I think about agentic workflows. The concept is genuinely revolutionary - persistent memory for coding agents that survives context compaction, dependency graphs, task tracking that actually works across sessions. But the internet is calling it a “virus.” And honestly? I agree with them. This isn’t a hit piece though. Steve Yegge didn’t deceive anyone. He was brutally upfront about the constraints. I just want to talk about what happens when good ideas meet messy execution, and the question I still can’t answer: when do you walk away?

item.title: Be careful with LLM "Agents" (Maurycy's blog)
item.description: I get it: Large Language Models are interesting... but you should not give "Agentic AI" access to your computer, accounts or wallet.

item.title: Why LLMs Can't Play Chess
item.description: LLMs' attempts to play chess are hilarious; they blunder queens and move illegally. Here's what this tells us about when to use LLMs to solve complex software engineering tasks, and when to absolutely not.

item.title: You don't have to if you don't want to.
item.description: "In the year 5555, your arms are hangin' limp at your sides, your legs got nothing to do, some machine doin' that for you." Zager and Evans sang these words in 1969. Now, thirty-five centuries ahead of schedule, it's never been easier to tell the world "I didn't care about this and I don't care about you."

item.title: The third hard problem
item.description: It’s not off-by-one errors.

item.title: Disposable Software: When generating code costs less than finding it
item.description: LLMs have made generating code nearly free, but maintaining it hasn't changed. On the growing plague of code that shouldn't exist and the discipline of searching before building.

item.title: What AI coding costs you | Tom Wojcik
item.description: What's the effect of the prolonged AI usage among coders and is it tracked correctly, if it all?

item.title: Headless Sync - Obsidian Help
item.description: Headless Sync - Obsidian Help

item.title: Don't trust AI agents | NanoClaw Blog
item.description: AI agents need a security model that assumes things will go wrong. The right response isn't better permission checks, it's architecture that makes trust unnecessary.

item.title: I Built an AI to Play Tempest… It Beat Me.
item.description:
I spent a year building an AI to master the hardest version of Tempest ever made… and yesterday it beat my official world record. What started as a stubborn engineering experiment turned into a full-blown machine learning breakthrough, complete with 15 million frames of gameplay, a custom MAME socket bridge, prioritized replay, and the two changes that finally cracked the plateau: attention and polar coordinates. Here’s how a 1-million-parameter model went from clueless to superhuman — and what it taught me about building AI that actually works.
Live Training Dashboard: http://davepl.com:8765
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Cognitive Debt: When Velocity Exceeds Comprehension | rockoder
item.description: A systems analysis of how AI-assisted development creates a gap between output speed and understanding, and why organizations cannot see it happening.

item.title: That's it, I'm cancelling my ChatGPT
item.description: OpenAI has decided to join the Department of war to enable mass surveillance and weapons deployment in war. We are all cancelling our OpenAI subscriptions.
